macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview of Nichsei Milog Usufruct Rights

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of Daf 80
Recap of previous discussion on tif of flocks and principal preservation
Introduction of Chanania's position on husband's rights to fruits

macro_segments.title: Classification of Fruits and Principal in Nichsei Milog: Goats, Hens, Trees, and Extractive Resources

micro_segments.title: Chanania's agreement that divorced wife can redeem offspring for payment and discussion of shevach beit aviha
Cases of goats for milk, sheep for wool, hens for eggs, palm trees for dates—regular fruit-bearing property
Garments worn away and salt/alum mines as fruits despite being non-renewable resources
Rabbi Meir versus Chachamim debate on whether fruits of trees are principal or fruits

macro_segments.title: Status Protection and Forced Sale: Old Slaves and Aged Orchards

micro_segments.title: Case of old slaves: husband can force sale unless rabban shimon ben gamliel invokes shevach beit aviha
Rabbi Yehuda's position protecting vineyard and olive groves despite poor production
Rav Kahana's distinction between wife's own field versus field she doesn't own regarding forced sale

macro_segments.title: Husband's Expenses on Wife's Property: Recovery Rights and Conditions

micro_segments.title: Basic rule: husband invests and eats—he bears profit and loss equally
If husband invested but never reaped any fruit: can take oath to recover investment
Gemara discussion on minimum amount of benefit (pressed fig, pressed date) to forfeit recovery rights

macro_segments.title: Oath Requirement and Preventing Fraud in Expense Recovery

micro_segments.title: Special case of minor wife (ktanah) capable of mi'un: husband's investment treated as third-party investment

macro_segments.title: Case Study: Inheritance Retrieval and Limitation on Recovery by Actual Increase in Value

micro_segments.title: Woman inherits property worth 400 zuz; husband spends 600 to retrieve it and 1 zuz for travel
Rav Ami's initial ruling that 1 zuz benefit means no recovery of investment
Rabbis' objection: principal retrieved is not peirot; hotzi v'lo achal applies
Rosh's limitation: cannot recover more than actual increase in property value

macro_segments.title: Oath Requirement and Preventing Fraud in Expense Recovery (continued)

micro_segments.title: Abaye: no oath needed if investment less than increase; Rava: always need oath to prevent deception
Resolution: oath always required; recovery limited to actual value increase, not full investment amount

macro_segments.title: Husband's Expenses on Wife's Property: Recovery Rights and Conditions (continued)

micro_segments.title: Sharecroppers on wife's field: deal made with husband, not with wife or land itself
Alternative view: sharecroppers deal with land itself if husband unable to work it

macro_segments.title: Husband's Right to Transfer Usufruct: Sale of Peirot Rights and Limitations

micro_segments.title: Question: can husband sell his peirot rights to a third party for lump sum?
Dispute between Yehuda Mar Bar Marmar (valid post-facto) and Rav Pappa (completely invalid)
Case of woman bringing maidservant; husband transfers her service to second wife—Rava's apparent leniency
Clarification: Rava's real position limits transfer to maintain revach beisa (household benefit), not for external sales

macro_segments.title: Reasons for Prohibition on Selling Peirot: Principal Protection Versus Household Income Maintenance

micro_segments.title: Abaye's reason: fear third-party buyer will exploit land and destroy principal
Rava's reason: peirot granted to maintain regular household income, not one-time lump sum
Distinctions limiting concern: land near city (oversight possible), husband is aris (knowledgeable), investment in bonds (ongoing yield)

topical_tags.term: Hotzi v'lo achal (invested but reaped nothing)
Rav Kahana distinction (own field vs. other's field)
